planting.note: Surface-sow fresh seeds in late summer or fall, or cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks and sow indoors in late winter; Himalayan blue poppy requires cool, humid conditions (Pacific Northwest, Scotland, high-altitude gardens) and struggles in hot, dry summers. Plants die after setting seed if allowed to flower in their first year — remove flower buds the first season to build strong clumps. Requires acidic, humus-rich, constantly moist but well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade in cool, humid climates; the Himalayan blue poppy hybrid (M. betonicifolia x M. grandis) is one of the most coveted and difficult plants in temperate horticulture — producing the most impossibly, unreasonably beautiful blue flowers imaginable, enormous (4–5 inch), sky-blue to deep cerulean-blue saucer-shaped poppies with a central boss of golden stamens, held on tall, leafy stems in late spring and early summer. The blue is not merely blue — it is a saturated, luminous, impossible clear blue that appears almost artificially colored to first-time viewers. Achieving this in the garden requires cool, humid summers (the plant's native habitat is the cloud forests of the Himalayas at 10,000–14,000 feet), acidic and constantly moist but excellently drained soil, and the patience to prevent flowering for the first year. In suitable cool, moist climates (Seattle, Scotland, high-altitude gardens) it is achievable; in hot, dry, or alkaline conditions it invariably fails. The cultivars 'Slieve Donard' and 'Lingholm' ('Blue Ice') are vigorous and perennial rather than monocarpic.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall or divide established mats in early spring; three-leaf bittercress spreads by creeping rhizomes and over time forms an effective, low groundcover in moist, shaded situations. It is semi-evergreen to fully evergreen in mild winters, maintaining its attractive dark-green, trifoliate leaves year-round. Best planted in drifts for maximum groundcover effect under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; three-leaf bittercress is a quietly attractive European woodland groundcover producing neat, dark-green, three-parted (trifoliate) leaves that have the quality of large, dark, clover-like foliage and are semi-evergreen through mild winters — forming a low carpet of attractive rounded leaflets that suppresses weeds and covers ground effectively under trees. In spring it produces a cloud of small, four-petaled, pure-white cruciform flowers on 4–6 inch stems that hover just above the foliage in a brief but cheerful display before the tree canopy closes above. The simple white flowers and clean, dark, rounded foliage make it quietly pleasing throughout the season. Spreads steadily by creeping rhizomes to form expanding patches — useful in competition with tree roots, where it can make progress without heavy soil disturbance. An underused woodland floor plant for temperate shade gardens, valuable for its evergreen quality and ability to spread slowly in competition with the root systems of large trees.

planting.note: Plant rhizome sections in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade; pinnate bittercress is a spring ephemeral — it emerges early, flowers, and begins going dormant by early summer. Mark the planting site to avoid disturbance. It spreads by brittle, easily-broken rhizome segments that are scattered around the planting site by digging — each fragment regenerates into a new plant.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; pinnate bittercress is a handsome spring-ephemeral from the beech forests of southern Europe, larger and more boldly architectural than the common weedy bittercresses — producing fresh, bright-green, pinnately-divided leaves with seven (or more) leaflets arranged along the stalk in the neatly ordered pattern characteristic of the genus, and in April through May producing substantial clusters of relatively large, four-petaled flowers in clear white to pale lilac-pink above the attractive foliage. The whole plant is a spring ephemeral — beginning to go dormant by early summer as the tree canopy closes above — but the spring show is attractive and the spreading rhizomes form an effective, gradually expanding colony in moist, shaded conditions. The brittle, white, tooth-like rhizomes break apart easily when disturbed, each fragment capable of regenerating a new plant — making it very easy to propagate by simply working the soil around established plants. Combines naturally with bluebells, wood anemones, and wild garlic in the spring woodland garden.

planting.note: Plant bulbils or rhizomes in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in shade under beech or other deciduous trees; coralroot rarely sets seed (it relies primarily on bulbil propagation) — collect the small, dark, rice-grain-sized bulbils from the leaf axils and plant immediately, or allow them to fall naturally and naturalize. Spring ephemeral: mark locations before the foliage disappears in summer.

directions: Plant bulbils in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full shade under beech or similar large deciduous trees; coralroot is a fascinating and unusual European spring woodland plant, historically classified as Dentaria bulbifera, that reproduces primarily by dropping tiny, dark, oval bulbils from the axils of the pinnate leaves rather than by seed — a vegetative propagation strategy adapted to the deep shade of beech forest floors where seed germination is unreliable. The plant produces handsome, pinnately-divided leaves with narrow leaflets and, in April and May, slender stems bearing loose clusters of pale-pink to lilac-pink, four-petaled flowers. The distinctive bulbils are clearly visible as small, dark, rice-like structures clustered at the base of each leaf through the spring season. Rarely found in cultivation despite its quiet charm; it is a true ancient-woodland indicator species in Britain and Europe, appearing almost exclusively in woodland with unbroken centuries of continuity. Outstanding for the specialist woodland garden under large, established beeches and other canopy trees where its preferred alkaline leaf-mold soil accumulates.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in cool weather or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; night phlox opens its flowers only at dusk and in overcast conditions, releasing its extraordinary fragrance — the flowers close tightly during daylight hours. Plant near pathways, seating areas, or bedroom windows to experience the nocturnal fragrance.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; night phlox is a remarkable South African native that has evolved a unique relationship with night-flying moth pollinators — the flowers close completely during the day and in bright sunlight (appearing as tight, white-edged, crimson-backed buds), then open at dusk to release one of the most extraordinarily powerful and sweet fragrances of any garden plant. The fragrance has been compared to honey, vanilla, almonds, and chocolate, and a single plant in full bloom can perfume an entire garden on a still evening. The opened flowers reveal five petals, white on the inner (upper) face, deep crimson to maroon on the outer (lower) face, creating a white flower with crimson reverse. A South African Cape native, it performs best in cool seasons and mild climates, blooming through spring and into summer before declining in heat. Plant directly beside a path, a garden bench, or beneath a bedroom window to enjoy the extraordinary nocturnal fragrance — during the day the plant with its closed flowers is unremarkable, but after dusk it transforms the garden into something extraordinary.
